attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a skin lesion on his left nasolabial fold that has been present for several months and is slowly growing in size. He reports occasional bleeding from the lesion. His medical history includes hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia, for which he takes regular medications. He is a non-smoker and works as an engineer on construction sites with significant sun exposure.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 1 cm raised, pearly lesion with visible telangiectasia at the inferior aspect of his left nasolabial fold (image).

This skin lesion was subsequently biopsied and proven to be basal cell carcinoma.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this basal cell carcinoma?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a nodular basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in a cosmetically sensitive area. The clinical features of a pearly appearance with telangiectasia are classic for nodular BCC. Given its location and characteristics, surgical excision is the most appropriate treatment. This allows for histological margin assessment and provides the highest cure rate. For facial BCCs, a margin of 3-4 mm is typically recommended.

Imiquimod is a topical immune response modifier that can be effective for superficial BCCs, particularly on the trunk or limbs. However, it is not first-line therapy for nodular BCCs on the face due to lower cure rates and the risk of significant local inflammation.

5-Fluorouracil is a topical chemotherapy agent that, like imiquimod, is primarily indicated for superficial BCCs. It is not appropriate for nodular BCCs as penetration into deeper tissues is limited.

Cryotherapy can be used for small, well-defined, low-risk BCCs. However, it is not recommended for facial lesions due to the risk of scarring and the inability to assess margins histologically.

Photodynamic therapy involves the application of a photosensitising agent followed by light exposure. While it can be effective for superficial BCCs, it is not recommended for nodular BCCs due to limited depth of penetration.

The choice of treatment for BCC depends on several factors including the location, size, and subtype of the tumour, as well as patient factors. Facial BCCs, particularly in cosmetically sensitive areas like the nasolabial fold, are best treated with surgical excision to ensure complete removal and optimal cosmetic outcome. Mohs micrographic surgery might be considered in some cases for optimal margin control.
learningPoints: Surgical excision with margin control is the gold standard treatment for facial basal cell carcinomas.
Non-surgical treatments are generally reserved for superficial basal cell carcinomas or when surgery is contraindicated.
Location, size, and subtype of basal cell carcinoma influence treatment choice.
references: Peris K, Fargnoli MC, Garbe C, Kaufmann R, Bastholt L, Seguin NB, Bataille V, Marmol VD, Dummer R, Harwood CA, Hauschild A, Höller C, Haedersdal M, Malvehy J, Middleton MR, Morton CA, Nagore E, Stratigos AJ, Szeimies RM, Tagliaferri L, Trakatelli M, Zalaudek I, Eggermont A, Grob JJ; European Dermatology Forum (EDF), the European Association of Dermato-Oncology (EADO) and the European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC). Diagnosis and treatment of basal cell carcinoma: European consensus-based interdisciplinary guidelines. Eur J Cancer. 2019 Sep;118:10-34. doi: 10.1016/j.ejca.2019.06.003. Epub 2019 Jul 6. PMID: 31288208.
